description: When 'which path' could mean two things, a speaker can use 'lilea'. That act makes existing wording or a situation less ambiguous. 'solae' (explain) builds understanding of why or how; clarification attends to what was unclear.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l' and release into high-front unrounded 'i'. Repeat the lateral contact before stressed true-mid front 'e'. Finish with open central 'a' as a separate hiatus syllable.
sound_symbolism: The lateral contact returns with a small change of vowel before the word opens into final 'a'. That repeated path can recall a speaker trying the wording again until the reference clears.
usage_notes: A statement or meaning can be the direct object, while a clause uses 'mena ... meno'. A beneficiary takes dative 'wei'. In noun position, 'lilea' is clarification.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Clarification gives confusion a chance to be repaired before it is treated as deceit or disagreement.

semantic_domains.cognition: ambiguity reduced in an existing meaning or situation
semantic_domains.communication: wording repaired so that its intended reference is easier to follow
